seoTitle: Add Tabs to a Confluence Page (2026 Layout Guide)
metaDescription: Learn how to add tabs to a Confluence page in 2026 using the native Tabs macro, so long documentation stays organized into clean, clickable sections.
intro: Long documentation pages become a wall of scrolling text without a way to group related content. This guide shows how to use the Tabs macro in Confluence to organize a page into clean, clickable sections in 2026.

steps.heading: Open the tabs macro
steps.body: In edit mode, type `/tabs` to bring up the tabbed interface container.

steps.heading: Title your first tab
steps.body: Click inside the first tab label to add a search-optimized title, then paste your core content.

steps.heading: Add more tabs
steps.body: Click the "+" icon on the macro control panel to add additional tab panes.

steps.heading: Fill each pane
steps.body: Drag and drop rich media, tables, or action items directly inside each tab window.

steps.heading: Pick a tab style
steps.body: Use the right-hand options drawer to choose between classic tabs or clean buttons.

steps.heading: Publish the page
steps.body: Click Publish to make the tabbed layout live for your shared documentation.

faq.question: Do I need a plugin to add tabs to a Confluence page?
faq.answer: The native Tabs macro handles most cases, though marketplace macros like Composition Tabs or Navitabs offer extra styling options.

faq.question: Can I reorder tabs after creating them?
faq.answer: Yes, the floating macro controls let you rearrange the tab order or remove unused tabs.

faq.question: What kind of content can go inside a tab?
faq.answer: You can drop text blocks, bullet lists, task lists, and full data tables inside individual tab panes.

faq.question: Will tabs work correctly on mobile?
faq.answer: It's a good idea to check your layout across mobile and desktop previews before publishing to confirm the tabs display properly.
